step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("Brachycephaly - cause")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on the extensive evidence gathered regarding brachycephaly, my revised stance aligns closer with option D: Coronbral suture fusion. Here’s why:
Firstly, the majority of literature points toward either bicornual (coronal + another suture) or unifocal (single suture) craniosynostosis as key contributors to brachycephaly. While sagittal suture involvement does contribute significantly to scaphocephaly, the direct link between premature fusion of the coronal sutures and brachycephaly is consistently emphasized. Secondly, many case reports highlight specific instances where corona-fusion alone resulted in brachycephaly-like symptoms, supporting the notion that this particular suture plays a pivotal role independent of others. Lastly, the differential diagnosis section mentions various syndromes linked exclusively to coronal suture involvement, reinforcing its singular significance in defining brachycephalic phenotypes.
Therefore, considering all presented data, I now firmly believe that premature fusion of the coronal sutures indeed causes brachycephaly. Hence,

Tool Call: retrieve("Brachycephaly - cause")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on the extensive evidence gathered regarding brachycephaly, my revised stance aligns closer with option D: Coronbral suture fusion. Here’s why:
Firstly, the majority of literature points toward either bicornual (coronal + another suture) or unifocal (single suture) craniosynostosis as key contributors to brachycephaly. While sagittal suture involvement does contribute significantly to scaphocephaly, the direct link between premature fusion of the coronal sutures and brachycephaly is consistently emphasized. Secondly, many case reports highlight specific instances where corona-fusion alone resulted in brachycephaly-like symptoms, supporting the notion that this particular suture plays a pivotal role independent of others. Lastly, the differential diagnosis section mentions various syndromes linked exclusively to coronal suture involvement, reinforcing its singular significance in defining brachycephalic phenotypes.
Therefore, considering all presented data, I now firmly believe that premature fusion of the coronal sutures indeed causes brachycephaly. Hence,
\boxed{D}
